executive_summary.executive_summary:
The Public Works Department continued to focus on core infrastructure maintenance, capital project delivery, and seasonal operations during the month. Street maintenance crews completed 12 lane-miles of asphalt overlay and 3.4 lane-miles of crack sealing, prioritizing collector streets with documented pavement condition index (PCI) scores below 60. The sidewalk repair team closed out 48 trip-hazard work orders, primarily in residential zones near schools and senior housing, and responded to 9 high-priority ADA accessibility complaints within the 10-business-day target.

Water utility staff completed the scheduled flushing of 18.7 miles of distribution mains in pressure zones 2 and 3, collected 126 bacteriological samples, and recorded no coliform-positive results. Two unplanned main breaks occurred (an 8" cast iron main on Oakwood Drive and a 6" ductile iron main on Martin Street); both were isolated and repaired within 6 hours with no documented property damage. Wastewater operations maintained regulatory compliance at the treatment plant, with average daily flow at 6.4 MGD (approximately 78% of permitted capacity) and no Sanitary Sewer Overflows (SSOs) reported.

The Facilities division completed preventive maintenance on 14 city buildings, including HVAC filter changes, elevator inspections, and emergency lighting checks. Energy-efficiency upgrades were initiated at the Community Center, including retrofitting 62 interior fixtures with LED lamps and installing occupancy sensors in five low-traffic rooms. The Fleet division placed three new single-axle dump trucks into service for the Streets division and decommissioned two high-mileage units, improving reliability for winter operations.

Major capital projects advanced on schedule. The Riverside Corridor Complete Streets Project reached 65% design, and the department hosted a well-attended public open house with over 70 residents providing input on bicycle facilities, crosswalk locations, and parking impacts. Construction on the Eastside Stormwater Improvements Project achieved substantial completion, with final punch-list items underway and initial field observations confirming improved drainage performance after two moderate storm events.

Customer service metrics remained strong: the Public Works service line received 412 calls and 163 online service requests, with 91% of requests closed within established service-level targets. The most common requests involved pothole repairs, streetlight outages, and illegal dumping. Staff continued to emphasize communication and transparency, including updating the online project dashboard and posting weekly construction status updates on the city website.

Overall, the month was characterized by steady progress on planned maintenance, proactive preparation for the upcoming winter season, and continued collaboration with other departments on cross-cutting initiatives such as emergency preparedness, sustainability, and long-range transportation planning.
executive_summary.interdepartmental_activity:
The Public Works Department worked closely with multiple city departments throughout the month to coordinate operations, support shared goals, and improve service delivery.

With the Planning and Community Development Department, staff participated in two joint technical review meetings for upcoming mixed-use developments along the Riverside Corridor. Public Works engineering staff provided detailed feedback on proposed driveway locations, sidewalk widths, stormwater management approaches, and utility connections. The teams collaborated to ensure that new developments align with the city’s Complete Streets policy and the recently adopted Stormwater Design Manual. Public Works also supplied updated traffic count data and turning-movement analyses to inform conditions of approval for the projects.

The department coordinated extensively with the Parks and Recreation Department on tree maintenance and shared-use paths. Streets and Parks staff met on-site at five locations where street trees were impacting sidewalks and curb lines; a joint plan was developed to balance pedestrian safety with tree health, including root pruning, selective removals, and replacement planting with more sidewalk-compatible species. Public Works also assisted Parks with minor grading and drainage improvements at two ballfields following drainage complaints from league users.

In collaboration with the Police and Fire Departments, Traffic Engineering staff participated in a monthly Traffic Safety Committee meeting to review collision data, near-miss reports from first responders, and citizen complaints. As a result, Public Works committed to installing additional reflective backplates at two high-incidence signalized intersections, refreshing crosswalk striping at three mid-block crossings near schools, and conducting a focused speed study along Maple Avenue in support of potential enforcement emphasis.

Public Works worked with the Emergency Management Office to update the city’s Debris Management Plan ahead of winter storm season. Operations supervisors provided equipment inventories, staffing patterns, and priority route maps, while Engineering staff contributed flood-prone area mapping and culvert capacity information. A joint tabletop exercise was held to test interdepartmental communication and resource deployment in a severe weather scenario, with follow-up action items identified for both Public Works and Emergency Management.

The department also coordinated with the Finance Department on the mid-year capital improvement program (CIP) budget review. Public Works project managers supplied updated cost estimates, construction schedules, and grant reimbursement status for all major projects. Finance staff and Public Works leadership worked together to reallocate underspent funds from two completed projects to address cost escalation on the Downtown Sewer Rehabilitation Project, ensuring continuity of construction without delay.

Finally, Public Works collaborated with the City Manager’s Office and the Communications team to prepare materials for a City Council study session on infrastructure funding needs. Staff contributed project photos, before-and-after examples of pavement rehabilitation, and explanatory graphics illustrating the long-term cost impacts of deferred maintenance. These interdepartmental efforts helped present a clear, unified message to elected officials and the public about current conditions, progress made, and ongoing challenges facing the city’s infrastructure system.

staff_report.total_staff:
As of 2025-11-30, the Public Works Department employed a total of 97 full-time equivalent (FTE) staff, distributed across four primary divisions:

- Administration and Engineering: 18 FTE
  - 1 Public Works Director
  - 1 Deputy Director/City Engineer
  - 3 Senior Civil Engineers
  - 5 Assistant/Associate Engineers
  - 2 Construction Inspectors
  - 2 GIS/Asset Management Specialists
  - 3 Administrative/Clerical Support Staff
  - 1 Project Accountant (shared with Finance, allocated at 0.5 FTE)

- Operations – Streets, Stormwater, and Traffic: 41 FTE
  - 1 Operations Manager
  - 3 Street Supervisors/Lead Workers
  - 18 Street Maintenance Workers (various levels)
  - 4 Traffic Signal Technicians
  - 3 Sign and Marking Technicians
  - 4 Stormwater Crew Members
  - 2 Right-of-Way Inspectors
  - 2 Equipment Operators (heavy equipment focus)
  - 4 Seasonal/temporary maintenance workers (averaging 0.5 FTE each over the month)

- Utilities – Water and Wastewater: 29 FTE
  - 1 Utilities Manager
  - 2 Water System Supervisors
  - 2 Wastewater Collection Supervisors
  - 8 Water Distribution Operators
  - 6 Wastewater Collection Operators
  - 4 Treatment Plant Operators
  - 2 Laboratory/Compliance Technicians
  - 2 Utility Service Technicians (metering and customer support)
  - 2 Maintenance Mechanics (shared between plant and lift stations)

- Facilities and Fleet: 9 FTE
  - 1 Facilities and Fleet Supervisor
  - 3 Facilities Maintenance Technicians
  - 3 Fleet Mechanics
  - 1 Parts/Inventory Specialist
  - 1 Custodial Lead (coordinates contracted custodial services)

In addition to these core staff, the department hosted one full-time engineering intern from a local university and one part-time administrative intern supporting customer service and records management functions. Overtime utilization was slightly above the year-to-date average due to after-hours water main repairs and extended shifts for night-time paving operations, but remained within the approved budget.

Overall, staffing levels are adequate to meet baseline service expectations, but there is limited redundancy in several specialized classifications (signal technicians, laboratory staff, and heavy-equipment operators), which creates some vulnerability when staff are out for training, illness, or vacation.
staff_report.staffing_needs:
The department continues to experience moderate but manageable staffing gaps, particularly in skilled technical and field positions. As of the end of the month, there are four vacant full-time positions:

1. Senior Civil Engineer (Capital Projects)
   - This position has been vacant for approximately four months following a retirement. The vacancy affects our ability to manage multiple large capital projects concurrently, including the Riverside Corridor Complete Streets Project and the Downtown Sewer Rehabilitation Project. Existing engineering staff are carrying higher-than-ideal project loads, which increases risk of schedule slippage and makes it harder to proactively pursue new grant opportunities.

2. Traffic Signal Technician II
   - The department currently has only three filled signal technician positions, which is thin given the size and complexity of the city’s signal network (74 signalized intersections, 12 school zone flashers, and multiple pedestrian-activated beacons). The vacancy has resulted in slightly longer response times for non-emergency signal issues and limits our capacity to implement signal timing updates and detection upgrades. Recruitment is underway, but the candidate pool has been relatively small due to regional competition for experienced technicians.

3. Water Distribution Operator I
   - This entry-level position supports routine valve exercising, hydrant maintenance, service line repairs, and meter-related work. The vacancy has required more overtime from existing staff to complete scheduled maintenance and respond to after-hours calls. While regulatory compliance is not currently at risk, the reduced staffing level leaves less flexibility to schedule training and cross-coverage for operators.

4. Facilities Maintenance Technician
   - With three technicians currently covering 14 city buildings totaling over 220,000 square feet, preventive maintenance tasks are being prioritized based on criticality, and some lower-priority cosmetic or minor repairs are experiencing delays. This vacancy also affects our capacity to support small in-house renovation and energy-efficiency projects, increasing our reliance on outside contractors for tasks that could otherwise be handled internally.

In addition to these vacancies, the department anticipates at least two retirements in the next 12–18 months (one senior equipment operator and one wastewater collection supervisor). Succession planning is underway, including identifying internal candidates for advancement, documenting key procedures, and scheduling cross-training.

From a broader staffing-needs perspective, the department has identified the following priority additions, contingent on budget approval:

- 1 additional Stormwater Crew Member to support increased inspection and maintenance demands associated with new stormwater regulations and the growth of green infrastructure installations (rain gardens, bioswales, permeable pavements).
- 1 Asset Management Analyst to help fully implement and utilize the computerized maintenance management system (CMMS), improve data quality, and support long-term capital planning based on asset condition and lifecycle analysis.

The department is working with Human Resources to refine job descriptions, adjust salary ranges where appropriate to remain competitive in the regional labor market, and broaden recruitment efforts through trade schools, professional associations, and targeted outreach to underrepresented groups. In the meantime, supervisors are carefully managing overtime, prioritizing critical work, and leveraging temporary staff and contractors where feasible to maintain service levels and meet regulatory obligations.

permit_summary_table.measure_description: Right-of-Way Excavation Permits (utility cuts, trenching, and pavement restoration in city streets)
Street Use / Lane Closure Permits (temporary closures for construction, moving, or special events)
Driveway and Curb Cut Permits (new or modified access to public streets)
Sidewalk and ADA Ramp Construction/Repair Permits (private frontage improvements impacting public right-of-way)
Utility Service Connection Permits (water, sewer, and storm service laterals in the public right-of-way)
Oversize/Overweight Vehicle Permits (single-trip and annual permits for large loads on city streets)
Encroachment Permits (signs, landscaping, and minor structures within public right-of-way)
